channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: South Korea’s Cherry Blossoms Attract a Connoisseur: The Japanese
item.description: Cherry blossom viewing, or hanami, is a cherished rite in Japan. Some Japanese are also doing it in South Korea.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Bulguksa Temple in Gyeongju, South Korea, in April.

item.title: Learning Muay Thai Can Allow Visitors to Stay in Thailand Longer
item.description: Visitors can apply for a five-year visa if they take lessons in “soft power” activities like the Thai martial art. Training does not have to be grueling, and sparring is optional.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Yong and Lauren DeCicca
item.media:description: Instructors leading a Muay Thai training session at a gym in Bangkok.

item.title: A 5-Month Wait and a 50% Fail Rate: It’s Hard to Get a British Driving License
item.description: The average wait for a test in England and Scotland has hit 22 weeks, up from five weeks before the pandemic, leading to frustration and attempts to jump the line.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Grace Reynolds, right, taking a driving lesson with Dean Batchelor in March in London. “It feels like I’ve got to pass — otherwise Christmas will be probably the next time,” Ms. Reynolds said.

item.title: Mexican Governor Accused by U.S. of Aiding Drug Cartel Steps Down
item.description: U.S. prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya of protecting the powerful Sinaloa Cartel in exchange for bribes and political support, setting off a national scandal.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of the Mexican state of Sinaloa, said he had decided to take “a temporary leave of absence.”

item.title: As Israel Entrenches, Frustration With Hezbollah Turns to Support
item.description: With the cease-fire fraying and Israel demolishing villages in the south, many Hezbollah supporters in Lebanon are putting aside their annoyance with the group and turning to it for protection.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum and David Guttenfelder
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Lithuania Says It Broke Up Russian Sabotage and Murder Plots
item.description: The arrests of nine people are reminders of the threat Russia poses in Europe at a time when Washington has shifted focus to the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Michael Schwirtz and Tomas Dapkus
item.media:description: Ruslan Gabbasov, who Lithuania says was the target of a Russian murder conspiracy, in Brussels in 2023.

item.title: U.S. Indictment of Sinaloa’s Governor Is a Reckoning for Residents of the Mexican State
item.description: The U.S. indictment of a Mexican governor confirms what many residents say they had long suspected: The line between organized crime and the upper echelons of government has blurred.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Mexican National Guard in Culiacán, Sinaloa, in February.

item.title: What the End of Saudi Arabia’s Big-Spending Era Means for the Kingdom and Beyond
item.description: The kingdom is pulling back from a costly and high-profile golf venture amid mounting financial concerns, raising questions about its plans.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A picture of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, in 2022.

item.title: Trump ‘Not Satisfied’ With Iran’s Proposal on Ending War
item.description: The details of the latest plan were unclear, and President Trump did not specify his objections.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A government-organized march in Tehran on Wednesday in support of Mojtaba Khamenei, the supreme leader.

item.title: Israel Said to Have Helped Defend Emirates in Iran War With Iron Dome
item.description: It appeared to be the first time the missile defense system was sent to an Arab country. The Emirates bore the brunt of Iran’s retaliatory attacks on Gulf countries during the war.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Missiles being intercepted over Tel Aviv last June.

item.title: Suspect in UK Stabbing Attack on Jewish Men Faces Third Attempted Murder Charge
item.description: A man appeared in court charged with the attempted murder of two Jewish men in Golders Green, in north London, and of another man, an acquaintance, elsewhere in the city.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Members of the Jewish community watched the police search the area after two people were stabbed in the Golders Green neighborhood of London on Wednesday.

item.title: In Packed Cafes, Iranians Yearn to Escape War’s Wreckage
item.description: Cafes and coffee shops give Iranians affordable places to talk about their hopes, fears and the cost of living as well as to seek company in uncertain times.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi and Arash Khamooshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Patrons at a cafe spilling out onto the sidewalk this month in Tehran.

item.title: How Indigenous Acknowledgments Became a Target in Australia
item.description: Recognition of Aboriginal Australians has become standard at most public events. Increasingly, the right is taking aim at the practice.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:description: Pastor Ray Minniecon speaking at the Anzac Day service in Sydney on Saturday.

item.title: How Germany Misjudged Trump’s Anger on Iran
item.description: After Chancellor Friedrich Merz upset President Trump with criticism of the war, he offered no public sign he believed Mr. Trump’s threats to pull troops were serious.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump speaking with Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany in the Oval Office in March.

item.title: Narges Mohammadi, Iranian Nobel Laureate, Is Hospitalized
item.description: Narges Mohammadi, a prominent human rights activist, was taken to a hospital after collapsing following a heart problem in prison.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Narges Mohammadi poses in an undated photo provided by her family. The Nobel laureate has suffered from heart ailments for years, according to her family.

item.title: Why U.S. Officials Are in Venezuela Right Now
item.description: U.S. officials visited Venezuela after the first direct flight between the two countries since 2019. Our White House correspondent Tyler Pager describes how the main message of the trip was, in the words of a White House adviser “drill, baby, drill.”
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Melanie Bencosme and Todd Heisler

item.title: On May Day Cuba’s Communist Leaders Deliver a Defiant Tone to the U.S.
item.description: In front of the American Embassy in Havana, the Cuban government held its annual International Worker’s Day celebration as the United States sought to end the communist ruling system.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
item.media:description: May Day celebrations took place in Havana early Friday morning.

item.title: Co-Founder of Superdry Fashion Brand Is Convicted of Rape
item.description: A British jury convicted James Holder, who rose to fame for his role in creating the streetwear brand, of raping a woman in May 2022.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Rod Minchin/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: James Holder was escorted from a courthouse in Cirencester, England, on Friday.

item.title: Very Dramatique! 4 Are Rescued After Trainee Driver Plunges Bus Into Seine
item.description: Nobody was hurt and all were rescued after a bus driver in training hit a parked car and veered into the river near Paris, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Divers working on a submerged bus in Juvisy-sur-Orge, near Paris, on Thursday.

item.title: War in Iran Is Making the Hajj, the Muslim Pilgrimage, More Expensive
item.description: Soaring jet fuel prices stemming from the war, the government of India said, have forced it to hike hajj package prices for its citizens.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim and Suhasini Raj
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The end of last year’s hajj pilgrimage in Mecca, Saudi Arabia.

item.title: Sofiane Pamart Emerged From France’s Rap Scene. Now, He’s Setting the Piano World on Fire.
item.description: Sofiane Pamart is on a mission to make his instrument as popular, and spectacular, as possible.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sofiane Pamart in Paris. “I want my piano to be unifying and to create bonds between all generations, all social classes,” he said.

item.title: Myanmar’s Junta Claims ‘Benevolence’ for Aung San Suu Kyi. It Still Rules Cruelly.
item.description: Moving the deposed civilian leader to a “designated residence” for the rest of her prison sentence is part of an effort by Myanmar’s regime to portray legitimacy.
item.dc:creator: Richard C. Paddock
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:credit: Myanmar Military Information Team, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: On Thursday, the state-run Myanmar Military Information Team released an image of Aung San Suu Kyi in an undisclosed location.

item.title: Violence Erupts in Australian Town After Arrest of Suspect in Girl’s Killing
item.description: The police in Alice Springs fired rubber bullets and tear gas to disperse an angry crowd demanding justice.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Rhett Hammerton/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: A police vehicle burning outside a hospital in Alice Springs, Australia, on Thursday.

item.title: What to Know About ‘Welcome to Country’ Ceremony Used by Indigenous Australians
item.description: The tradition, dating back thousands of years, is used by Indigenous Australians to greet visitors to their ancestral lands.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:description: Joy Murphy Wandin, an Indigenous elder, delivering the “Welcome to Country” before a rugby game in Melbourne last year.

item.title: In Bulgaria, a New Government Challenges an Old Puppet Master
item.description: Delyan Peevski was a focus of protests that led to Bulgarian elections. Can a new government shake his hold on power?
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.category: Movement for Rights and Freedoms (Bulgarian Political Party)
item.media:description: Delyan Peevski greeting supporters in Sofia, Bulgaria, in 2024. To his critics, Mr. Peevski has come to embody Bulgaria’s failure to establish the rule of law.

item.title: Oil Companies’ Huge Profits Revive Calls for Temporary Windfall Taxes
item.description: European nations imposed temporary taxes in the 2022 energy shock when Russia invaded Ukraine, but whether they can effectively help households is up for debate.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Exxon Mobil refinery in Texas. The United States has not been hit as hard by energy price increases as countries in Asia and Europe.

item.title: Why Countries Are Stocking Up on Gold
item.description: As risk has escalated, central banks have bought more gold to stash in reserve. A widening Middle East war could add to the urgency.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

item.title: Where in the World Is All That Gold Stored?
item.description: As central banks buy more gold, where to put all that heavy metal is an increasingly important question. Reserves must be secure and ready to trade in a crisis.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: More than 60 central banks store gold at the Bank of England.

item.title: Nigerian Military Runs a Fulani ‘Concentration Camp,’ Group Says
item.description: Amnesty International said about 1,500 people have been detained by the military for three months and that many of those who have died from disease and starvation have been children.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh and Ismail Auwal
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Soldiers patrolling in a village in Kwara State, Nigeria, in February. The state has seen a surge in violent attacks in recent months.

item.title: Hezbollah Using Fiber-Optic Drones Against Israeli Targets
item.description: The Lebanese militant group is attacking Israeli troops with explosive drones controlled by fiber-optic cables, like those commonly used in the war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner, Natan Odenheimer, Aaron Boxerman and Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An Israeli military artillery unit on the Israeli side of the border with Lebanon on Thursday.

item.title: Decoding the King: Brits Hear Subtle Rebuke to Trump that Americans Might Miss
item.description: Royal watchers in Britain called the visit of King Charles III to America a master class in understated criticism.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: President Trump with King Charles III at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: The Future of Apple
item.description: Tim Cook made Apple rich. The question for his successor is whether he can make it cool again.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: New Banksy Statue Causes Stir in Central London
item.description: The statue depicts a man marching with a flag that covers his face. It appeared in a section of London near statues of 19th-century British military and colonial figures.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: The artist Banksy appeared to have taken credit for statue of a man holding a flag covering his face that stood in Waterloo Place in London.

item.title: After Seven Years, First U.S. Direct Flight Lands in Caracas
item.description: President Trump banned commercial flights to Venezuela during his first term, but the flights are the latest step in re-establishing ties between the two countries.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:description: The first direct flight between the United States and Venezuela landed in Caracas on Thursday afternoon.

item.title: Sheinbaum’s Dilemma in Mexico: Defy the U.S. or Arrest an Ally
item.description: The Trump administration wants President Claudia Sheinbaum to arrest a Mexican governor. She is faced with few good options in response.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico could arrest one of her party allies, the governor of Sinaloa state, and extradite him to the United States, or defy the U.S. request and protect him.

item.title: The World Met to Talk Climate Change. The U.S. Wasn’t Invited.
item.description: Dozens of countries met this week to discuss how to end the world’s dependence on fossil fuels, a goal that the Trump administration doesn’t share.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Delegates gathered for a meeting on transitioning away from fossil fuels in Santa Marta, Colombia, on Wednesday.

item.title: Daw Aung San Suu Kyi Is Moved to House Arrest From Prison in Myanmar
item.description: The ousted civilian leader has been detained since a military coup forced her from power in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:description: Protesters with a banner of Daw Aung San Suu Kyi in Yangon, Myanmar, in 2021. Her family has called for her release, warning that her health was deteriorating.

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Signals Plan to Maintain Control Over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei said in a statement that Iran would establish “new legal frameworks” for the Strait of Hormuz. He also said his country would retain its nuclear capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians held photos of the country’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, at a government-organized march in Tehran on Wednesday.

item.title: How Trump’s Iran Blockade Is Complicating a High-Stakes Trip to China
item.description: If President Trump flies to China as planned in May, the primary topic will clearly be the rippling economic effects of a war that Beijing has made clear it viewed as unnecessary.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran last week showing Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz. China imports about a third of its oil and gas through the waterway.

item.title: August Wilson Like You’ve Never Heard Him Before: In Italian
item.description: In three U.S. cities, a new production of the playwright’s cabdriver drama “Jitney” will be imported from Italy.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Maurizio Fiorino
item.media:credit: Maurizio Fiorino for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rosanna Sparapano and Miguel Gobbo Diaz, actors in a new revival of “Jitney,” at the final dress rehearsal of the Sardegna Teatro production in Sardinia, Italy.

item.title: Journalist Detained in Kuwait Says He Was Stripped of Citizenship
item.description: Ahmed Shihab-Eldin, a Kuwaiti-American, was held for several weeks after posting about the Iran war. Kuwait does not appear to have commented publicly on his case.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ahmed Shihab‑Eldin in Doha, Qatar, last year.

item.title: What to Know About the Stabbing Attack Against 2 Jewish Men in London
item.description: The British police said the attack on Wednesday was being treated as terrorism, and they warned of rising antisemitic hate crimes.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: A crime scene investigator at the site of a stabbing attack in the Golders Green neighborhood of London on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump May Pull Troops From Germany. That Isn’t as Scary as It Once Was.
item.description: Germany has hugely increased its military spending, aiming to be less dependent on Washington. Its support for U.S. attacks on Iran may also give it leverage.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley, Christopher F. Schuetze and Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: American soldiers in Germany last month. On Wednesday, President Trump declared that the United States was “studying and reviewing the possible reduction of Troops in Germany.”

item.title: Missing Indigenous Girl in Australia Is Found Dead After 5-Day Search
item.description: The police are seeking a man who was last seen holding hands with the 5-year-old girl on Saturday night near Alice Springs.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Rhett Hammerton/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: Volunteers and officials searching for a missing five-year-old girl in Alice Springs, Australia, on Tuesday.

item.title: Korean Air Bans Roosters on U.S. Flights to the Philippines
item.description: The airline was used by chicken breeders in Texas to illegally transport roosters for cockfights, an animal welfare group said.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:description: An animal welfare group said that roosters were being shipped from the United States to the Philippines on Korean Air flights.

item.title: Ukraine Reacts With Shrug to Phone Call Between Trump and Putin
item.description: More than a year of similar conversations have failed to bring the country any closer to peace, so Ukrainians have stopped hoping they will.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia and President Trump in Anchorage in August. On Wednesday, the two leaders spoke by phone for the first time this year.

item.title: Attacks on Jewish Targets in Europe Suggest Hybrid Warfare
item.description: Officials are investigating similar attacks across Europe, all claimed by a shadowy Islamist group that may be using low-cost, unsophisticated methods to sow fear in Jewish communities.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia and Koba Ryckewaert
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An arson attack in the Golders Green neighborhood of London in March destroyed four ambulances run by a Jewish organization.

item.title: Israel Intercepts Aid Flotilla Heading to Breach Naval Blockade of Gaza
item.description: Activists said the Israeli military boarded and disabled boats carrying humanitarian assistance to the territory in international waters near Greece.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner, Lynsey Chutel and Rawan Sheikh Ahmad
item.media:description: Boats carrying activists and humanitarian aid bound for Gaza, as part of the Global Sumud Flotilla, in Barcelona, Spain, this month.

item.title: Iranians Live With Pain and Powerlessness, Beneath a Smooth Veneer
item.description: After months of upheaval, many are attempting to get on with their lives while quietly grappling with grief, economic stress and a loss of hope.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kiana Hayeri for The New York Times
item.media:description: An emotional goodbye on Iran’s border with Turkey before an Iranian family headed to Australia for work, not knowing when they would reunite with loved ones at home.

item.title: How the War in Iran is Affecting Africa’s Economy and Fuel Supply
item.description: Despite the fragile cease-fire in the Middle East, many Africans say they are bracing for tougher times ahead and making difficult decisions about the future.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon, Golden Matonga and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A girl using a solar-powered light to study during electricity rationing in Juba, South Sudan, last month.

item.title: Iceland’s Pools and Hot Tubs Are Now UNESCO-Recognized. Some Locals Aren’t Thrilled.
item.description: Iceland’s swimming pools and hot tubs serve as hubs of social life, a cultural distinction recently honored by UNESCO. Some Icelanders aren’t so thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: Police in Australia Were Warned of Terror Risk Before Bondi Attack, Report Says
item.description: A Jewish security group told police an attack on the community was “likely” because of heightened antisemitism, days before December’s mass shooting in Sydney.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim and Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memorial at Bondi Beach, the site of the attack, in Sydney, Australia, in December.

item.title: Rising Fuel Prices Force Policymakers to Weigh Excruciating Choices
item.description: The Bank of England and European Central Bank held interest rates steady on Thursday, as officials search for signs of possible longer-term damage and warn of the impact of a prolonged energy shock.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Paris. The war in Iran had an almost immediate impact on European inflation, increasing gasoline prices as well as airfares and other fuel-intensive activities.

item.title: Mother Runs for Office in India After Daughter Was Raped and Killed
item.description: Ratna Debnath is a candidate for Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s party, which is campaigning on women’s safety in an Indian state election.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das and Hari Kumar
item.media:credit: Atul Loke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ratna Debnath, center, campaigning in the North 24 Parganas district in West Bengal this month.

item.title: In France, Trump’s Triumphal Arch Draws Eye Rolls
item.description: The Arc de Triomphe in Paris, which the president vows to top in Washington, offers a lesson in the complicated history of monuments.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Conceived in 1806 by Napoleon, the Arc de Triomphe has come to symbolize very different things in modern France.

item.title: British Royals Crisscross Manhattan in Brief Visit Packed With Photo Ops
item.description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla laid flowers at the Sept. 11 memorial before stopping by an urban farm, the New York Public Library, a business event and a gala.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear, Claire Fahy and Sarah Lyall
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Michael Bloomberg, left, King Charles III and Queen Camilla laid flowers at the 9/11 memorial on Wednesday morning, the first of several stops during the royal couple’s visit to New York City.
